method: Analyzes circulating tumor cells from peripheral blood using epigenetically activated medium for cell stabilization, followed by fluorophore-conjugated antibody characterization against multiple immunocytochemistry markers; quantitative fluorescence imaging on high-content screening platform
cancerTypesNotes: 70+ cancer types across major histological subtypes including AD (bile duct, breast, colon, lung, ovary, pancreas, prostate, etc.), SCC (oral, lung, cervix, skin, etc.), NET, GIST, melanoma, mesothelioma, CNS tumors, and TCC
targetPopulation: Asymptomatic individuals
sensitivityNotes: 88.24% sensitivity in multi-cancer validation study; based on detection of circulating tumor-associated cell clusters
specificityNotes: 96.3% specificity in clinical validation
performanceNotes: Validated across multiple peer-reviewed publications; hallmark CTC clusters signify 230x higher one-year cancer risk
fdaStatusNotes: CE-IVD marked in Europe; not FDA-cleared in US
reimbursementNote: Coverage varies by country; primarily self-pay
clinicalAvailability: Commercially available (CE-IVD)
tatNotes: 14 working days from sample receipt
listPriceNotes: Contact vendor for pricing
numPublicationsNotes: Akolkar et al. Int J Cancer 2019 (circulating tumor-associated cell ensembles); Gaya et al. Cancer Cytopathol 2020 (pan-cancer diagnostic triaging); Ranade et al. Cancer Prev Res 2021 (230x one-year cancer risk)
validationCohortStudy: Multi-center validation across >40,000 participants per vendor; peer-reviewed studies include diagnostic triaging cohort
regulatoryStatusNotes: CE-IVD marked; uses CTC approach distinct from ctDNA-based MCED tests
technologyDifferentiator: CTC-based approach (not ctDNA) - analyzes intact circulating tumor cells rather than fragmented DNA; enables detection of tumor-associated cell clusters which correlate with higher cancer risk; 70+ cancer type coverage using immunocytochemistry markers

name: OncoXPLORE+
method: Integrates three parallel analytical tracks from a single blood sample: (1) 441-gene targeted panel covering 1.5 Mb including all exonic regions and 20bp flanking regions for SNVs, Indels, fusions, and splicing variants; (2) genome-wide Copy Number Alteration (CNA) profiling via LP-WGS; (3) GIPXplore fragmentomics module using proprietary ML pipeline analyzing genome-wide cfDNA fragmentation signatures for cancer detection and Tissue-of-Origin inference. Built on 15+ years of KU Leuven research.
cancerTypesNotes: Pan-cancer detection capability; fragmentomics module can infer Tissue-of-Origin for localization. Specific validation data for colorectal cancer, breast cancer, and multiple myeloma.
targetPopulation: High-risk asymptomatic individuals, NSAS triage, therapy selection when tissue unavailable
sensitivityCitations: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46435-0 | Vendor data (Christophe Van Huffel, OncoDNA, Jan 2026)
sensitivityNotes: ~53.5% sensitivity for early-stage cancers at ~99% specificity in asymptomatic screening context. GIPXplore classifier achieved 81.62% overall accuracy with 95% specificity for pan-cancer detection. Cancer-specific AUCs from Nature Comms study: Multiple Myeloma 95.0%, Early-Stage Breast Cancer 90.1%, Colorectal Cancer 84.7%.
specificityCitations: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-46435-0 | Vendor data (Christophe Van Huffel, OncoDNA, Jan 2026)
specificityNotes: ~99% specificity in early-stage screening context. 95% specificity for pan-cancer GIPXplore classifier. Near 100% specificity for all variant types on targeted panel.
tissueOfOriginNotes: 88.7% TOO accuracy for true positives using fragmentomics-based cell type deconvolution. Leverages cell-type-specific epigenetic patterns; can identify contributions from 490+ cell types.
lodNotes: Analytical LoD near 0.3% VAF for targeted 441-gene panel. Technical sensitivity: 88.6% at 0.5% VAF, >93% at 1% VAF, >93% at 2% VAF.
lodCitations: Vendor data (Christophe Van Huffel, OncoDNA, Jan 2026)
genesAnalyzedNotes: 441-gene targeted panel covering 1.5 Mb content. Detects SNVs, Indels, fusions, and unusual splicing. Complemented by genome-wide LP-WGS for CNA profiling.
genesAnalyzedCitations: https://oncodna.com | Vendor data (Christophe Van Huffel, OncoDNA, Jan 2026)
fdaStatusNotes: Currently Research Use Only (RUO) kit for collaborative projects. CE-IVDR technical file submission planned for end of 2026. One of only 2 finalists in EU OncNGS Pre-Commercial Procurement project.
reimbursementNote: RUO product; no reimbursement pathway currently
clinicalAvailability: Early Access Programme (EAP) from March 2026 for collaborative projects; Phase 3 OncNGS clinical validation at 7 European centers
tatNotes: 4-7 days from blood draw to actionable results
clinicalTrials: OncNGS Phase 3 clinical validation at 7 European pilot hospitals including Institut Curie (France), Ludwig Maximilians University (Germany), Institut Jules Bordet (Belgium)
validationCohortStudy: Nature Communications 2024 study: 744 healthy individuals and patients (Stanley et al.)
numPublicationsNotes: Stanley/Vermeesch et al., Nature Communications 2024 (cell type signatures in cfDNA fragmentation); Passemiers/Vermeesch et al., Bioinformatics 2024 (MetDecode methylation-based deconvolution). Additional OncNGS consortium publications in progress.
regulatoryStatusNotes: RUO product in OncNGS Phase 3 clinical validation (through March 2026). CE-IVDR submission planned end of 2026. Integrated into OncoKDM platform (registered medical device, GDPR compliant).
technologyDifferentiator: Multi-modal liquid biopsy combining mutations + CNV + fragmentomics in single workflow. GIPXplore fragmentomics leverages cell-type-specific epigenetic patterns to detect cancer signals even when specific mutations are unknown or at very low concentrations. Tumor-naïve approach - no prior tissue biopsy required. Built on 15+ years of KU Leuven research. One of only 2 finalists in EU OncNGS Pre-Commercial Procurement.
vendorRequestedChanges: 2026-01-08: Initial submission by Christophe Van Huffel (OncoDNA) with detailed vendor responses to clarification questions. Performance data from Nature Communications 2024 and internal validation cohorts. 2026-01-20: Updated availability (EAP March 2026) and TAT (4-7 days) per press release.

name: MethylScan HCC
method: DNA methylation biomarkers via targeted multiplexed NGS; 5 CpG sites (HCC-detect: CHFR, VASH2, CCNJ, GRID2IP + HCC-spec: F12)
methodNotes: Bisulfite conversion followed by targeted NGS. HCC-detect panel (4 CpGs) identifies hepatocyte-derived cfDNA; HCC-spec marker (F12 gene) distinguishes HCC from benign liver disease. Combined test branded as 'epiLiver' in validation study.
targetPopulation: Adults at high risk for HCC including those with cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis B/C, or other liver disease
sensitivityNotes: 84.5% sensitivity at 95% specificity threshold (n=554 clinical validation in Dhaka). AUC 0.94. Performance maintained across all BCLC stages. Superior to AFP alone (85% vs 61% sensitivity).
stageISensitivityNotes: 73% sensitivity for BCLC Stage A (early-stage) HCC. Some sources cite 75%. Stage-independent detection due to categorical (not quantitative) methylation differences.
specificityNotes: 92% specificity in clinical validation. Study also reported 95% specificity at different threshold. High specificity for HCC vs other cancer types (tested against 31 cancer types in TCGA, n=8,754).
ppvNotes: PPV not reported in primary validation study
npvNotes: NPV not reported in primary validation study
tatNotes: Estimated turnaround time
fdaStatusNotes: Available as laboratory-developed test through CLIA/CAP accredited laboratory
reimbursement: Has PLA code and CMS pricing determination; working with MolDx for LCD reimbursement
reimbursementNote: Coverage emerging through Medicare LCD pathway
clinicalAvailability: Available via test requisition form or physician portal
listPriceNotes: Contact vendor for pricing
clinicalTrials: Clinical validation study (n=554) in Dhaka, Bangladesh
numPublicationsNotes: Zhang et al. Nature Communications 2023 - 'A high-throughput test enables specific detection of hepatocellular carcinoma'
validationCohortStudy: Prospective clinical validation in high-HCC-burden population (Dhaka, Bangladesh): 302 HCC patients (BCLC A=34, B=86, C=106, D=76), 46 controls, 49 CHB, 102 non-HCC cancers, 50 healthy plasma
regulatoryStatusNotes: CLIA LDT with PLA code. Available for clinical ordering.
technologyDifferentiator: Methylation-based approach detects HCC-specific epigenetic changes rather than mutations. Markers are categorically methylated in HCC (binary signal) vs unmethylated in normal tissue, providing robust detection even at low tumor burden. Unique specificity for HCC vs other liver conditions and other cancer types.
vendorRequestedChanges: 2026-01-12: Initial submission by Lara Baden (EarlyDx) with performance data from Nature Communications 2023. Commercial availability confirmed with PLA code.

coveredIndications: Adults at high risk for HCC (cirrhosis, chronic hepatitis B/C, liver disease)
medicareCoverage.notes: Has CPT PLA code, working with MolDx for LCD coverage. Published in Nature Communications 2023.

name: SPOT-MAS
method: Multi-omics analysis integrating ctDNA methylomics, fragmentomics, genomics, and end motifs with AI/machine learning for cancer detection and tissue-of-origin localization
cancerTypesNotes: Detects 10 major cancer types and 75 subtypes; strong performance in cancers lacking standard screening (stomach, liver-biliary, ovary, pancreas)
sensitivityNotes: 70.8% overall sensitivity for cancerous lesions, 78.1% when including precancerous lesions in digestive tract; Stage I: 62.3%, Stage II: 73.9%, Stage IIIA: 88.3%
specificityNotes: K-DETEK trial (n=9,024)
stageISensitivityNotes: Discovery cohort all-cancer Stage I sensitivity: 73.9%. Validation: 73.91% (n=23). K-DETEK prospective: 70.59% early/non-metastatic.
ppvNotes: From K-DETEK interim analysis
tumorOriginAccuracyNotes: 70% tissue-of-origin accuracy in eLife case-control. K-DETEK prospective: 84% TOO accuracy.
fdaStatusNotes: US validation underway with Pangea Laboratory; available as LDT in Asian markets
reimbursementNote: No US coverage; available in Southeast Asia
clinicalAvailability: Commercial in Asian markets (Vietnam, Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, Indonesia, Philippines); Turkey launch announced Feb 2026 with real-world evidence (n=12,281: 78.2% sensitivity, 99.8% specificity); US validation underway with Pangea Laboratory
clinicalTrials: K-DETEK trial
numPublicationsNotes: eLife (case-control, n=2,288), BMC Medicine (K-DETEK trial), ESMO Annals of Oncology (real-world, n=10,577)
performanceNotes: eLife case-control (n=2,288): 72.4% sensitivity, 97% specificity across breast, CRC, gastric, liver, lung. K-DETEK prospective (n=9,057): 70.83% sensitivity, 99.71% specificity, 58.1% PPV, 99.92% NPV. Cost-effective shallow sequencing (~0.55x) enables population-wide screening.
technologyDifferentiator: Multimodal cfDNA analysis combining 5 feature types (targeted methylation, genome-wide methylation, fragment length, copy number, end motifs) in a single low-cost workflow. Shallow sequencing depth (~0.55x) vs competitors requiring 30x+, enabling significantly lower cost per test. First MCED test clinically validated in a large prospective Asian cohort.
perCancerEarlyStageSensitivitySource: eLife 2023 validation cohort, Table S8 (Nguyen et al., doi:10.7554/eLife.89083). Stage I-II combined. Lung excluded (n=3). Gastric n=7 (small sample).
regulatoryStatusNotes: CAP-accredited NGS laboratories in Singapore and Vietnam
vendorRequestedChanges: 2026-01-22: Initial submission by David Schlesinger (Gene Solutions)

name: ClarityDX Prostate
method: Combines blood-based biomarkers (PSA, free PSA) with clinical information using optimized machine learning algorithms to generate a risk score for clinically significant prostate cancer (grade group ≥2). Uses gradient-boosting machine algorithm.
cancerTypesNotes: Clinically significant prostate cancer (grade group ≥2). For men 40-75 years with PSA ≥3 ng/mL and clinical suspicion (elevated PSA, abnormal DRE, symptoms). No prior prostate cancer diagnosis.
sampleCollectionNotes: Blood sample, spun down. Stable 5 days at 2-8°C, 24h at room temp. Freeze at -20°C after centrifugation.
requiresTumorTissueNotes: Non-invasive blood test used before biopsy decision
sensitivityNotes: At ≥25% risk threshold. Validation cohort n=1,257. Higher than PSA or %free PSA alone.
specificityNotes: At ≥25% threshold. Could avoid up to 35% of unnecessary biopsies.
ppvNotes: At ≥25% threshold in validation cohort
fdaStatusNotes: Not FDA cleared. CLIA-certified, CAP-accredited lab in Orlando, FL.
reimbursementNote: PLA code granted by AMA (2025). Coverage varies by payer.
clinicalAvailability: Commercially available

numPublicationsNotes: Nature npj Digital Medicine 2024 (n=3,448 from UCLA, Johns Hopkins, U Calgary, U Alberta, Thomayer U Hospital).
regulatoryStatusNotes: CLIA LDT via Protean BioDx (US) and Nanostics (Canada). Original test developed by Nanostics Inc.
screeningPopulation: Men with elevated PSA (≥3 ng/mL) or abnormal DRE considering biopsy
intendedUse: Reflex test after elevated PSA to help determine need for prostate biopsy

name: OnkoSkan
method: Sandwich ELISA assay (DxPG80) measuring hPG80 (human circulating progastrin) in EDTA plasma. hPG80 is produced by cancer cells and correlates with tumor burden. No cross-reactivity to other gastrin peptides.
cancerTypesNotes: Pan-cancer biomarker applicable to 16+ cancer types. GAST gene (encoding progastrin) is activated across multiple tumor origins. For non-pregnant adults 18+.
sampleCollectionNotes: Frozen plasma required. Store at -20°C or 2-8°C. Do not freeze whole blood.
sensitivityNotes: Median hPG80 in cancer patients: 4.88 pM vs 1.05 pM in healthy donors (p<0.0001). Detected in 84% of HCC patients.
specificityNotes: High analytical specificity - no cross-reactivity with other gastrin peptides. No interference from PPI usage.
lodNotes: LoD: 1 pM. LoQ: 3.3 pM. Linear 0-50 pM.
fdaStatusNotes: Not FDA cleared. DxPG80 assay by ECS-Progastrin (Switzerland), offered via Protean BioDx.
reimbursementNote: Self-pay or clinical trial
clinicalAvailability: Commercially available
tatNotes: 2-5 business days
numPublicationsNotes: EBioMedicine 2020 (n=1,546, 11 cancer types). NENs 2022 (n=95). HCC prognosis 2022 (n=168).
regulatoryStatusNotes: DxPG80 kit by ECS-Progastrin. Offered as LDT via Protean BioDx.
screeningPopulation: Adults 18+ with suspected cancer or for treatment response monitoring
intendedUse: Cancer screening and treatment response monitoring via circulating progastrin levels

name: Caris Detect
approach: Blood-based cfDNA MCED (plasma) via extreme ultra-deep Whole Genome Sequencing + AI
method: Ultra-deep Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS) combined with AI/machine-learning models trained on 1M+ molecular profiles and 50B+ molecular markers; analyzes mutations, epigenomic changes, transcriptomic patterns, and aneuploidy from cfDNA; does not require prior tissue biopsy.
methodNotes: Caris Detect is a distinct product from Caris Assure (which is commercially available for therapy selection in advanced cancers). The July 2025 Caris Assure Scientific Reports paper demonstrated MCED capability using the Assure platform with DIFFERENT methodology and performance numbers (83-96% stage I-IV sensitivity at 99.6% specificity). Those numbers apply to Caris Assure, NOT Caris Detect. Do not conflate the two products.
cancerTypes: Multi-cancer; specific cancer type list not yet disclosed
cancerTypesNotes: Achieve 1 interim does not disclose the full list of cancer types evaluated. Cancer-type-specific Stage I+II sensitivity from Q4 2025 earnings slides: lung 87%, prostate 79%, head & neck 100% — other cancer types not disclosed. The Caris Assure platform (separate product) covers solid tumor types broadly, but the Caris Detect cancer panel may differ.
targetPopulation: Screening and symptomatic screening adults
perCancerEarlyStageSensitivitySource: Achieve 1 finalized results (Caris Life Sciences, March 31, 2026; interim Feb 2026)
sensitivityNotes: FINALIZED Achieve 1 results (March 31, 2026; n=3,014 evaluable subjects): Overall all-stages sensitivity not separately reported by Caris; they report ~60.3% as combined Stage I-II sensitivity. This is NOT comparable to the overall sensitivity field used for other MCED tests (e.g., Galleri 51.5% overall, Cancerguard 64% overall, Shield MCD 60% overall). Breakdown: Stage I 56.8%, Stage IV 98.6%. Stage III not separately reported. Cancer-type-specific Stage I+II sensitivity from earlier earnings slides: lung 87%, prostate 79%, head & neck 100%. CRITICAL CAVEATS: (1) Finalized cross-validation data; blinded validation results may differ. (2) Enriched cohort — the undiagnosed population had ~7% subsequent cancer diagnosis rate, substantially higher than general screening prevalence (~0.5-1%). (3) Many cancer-specific sample counts are small, reducing precision for individual cancer type estimates. Updated from interim readout (n=2,122; Feb 26, 2026).
sensitivityType: Stage I-II combined only (~60.3% finalized; overall all-stages sensitivity not reported)
stageISensitivityNotes: Achieve 1 finalized results (March 31, 2026), cross-validation, n=3,014 total evaluable subjects. Stage I sensitivity unchanged from interim.
stageIISensitivityNotes: From Achieve 1 interim readout (Feb 2026). Finalized press release reports combined Stage I-II ~60.3% but does not separately report Stage II. Retaining interim figure pending specific finalized Stage II data.
stageIIISensitivityNotes: Stage III sensitivity not separately reported in Achieve 1 finalized results. Sensitivity ranges from 56.8% (Stage I) to 98.6% (Stage IV) without explicit Stage III breakout.
stageIVSensitivityNotes: Achieve 1 finalized results (March 31, 2026), n=3,014 evaluable subjects. Stage IV sensitivity slightly lower than interim (99.1%).
specificityNotes: FINALIZED Achieve 1 results (March 31, 2026; n=3,014): TWO SPECIFICITY VALUES REPORTED: (1) 99.2% in the Asymptomatic Screening Population — subjects with no symptoms, no significant cancer risk factors, and no subsequent cancer diagnosis within 1 year of blood draw. (2) 96.0% in the broader Benign/High-Risk Population. The 99.2% figure is used as the primary specificity as it most closely reflects a true asymptomatic screening use case. Updated from interim (99.1% asymptomatic / 95.3% undiagnosed). For comparison: Galleri 99.5% (n=6,290 non-cancer), Shield MCD 98.5%, Cancerguard 97.4%.
performanceNotes: STUDY DESIGN: Achieve 1 FINALIZED results (March 31, 2026). Total n=3,014 evaluable subjects (up from 2,122 interim). METHODOLOGY: Cross-validation approach. COHORT ENRICHMENT: The undiagnosed population was enriched for higher-risk subjects — among ~600 subjects with 1-year follow-up, ~7% were subsequently diagnosed with cancer, well above general screening prevalence. FINALIZED vs INTERIM: Stage I sensitivity unchanged (56.8%), Stage IV slightly lower (98.6% vs 99.1%), specificity slightly improved (99.2% vs 99.1% asymptomatic; 96.0% vs 95.3% benign/high-risk). TECHNOLOGY: Caris claims superior early-stage sensitivity vs methylation-only MCED approaches (Galleri, Shield MCD) due to multi-modal genomic analysis covering mutations + epigenomics + transcriptomics + aneuploidy via extreme ultra-deep WGS. An updated assay additionally incorporating Whole Transcriptome Sequencing (WTS) is in development. RELATIONSHIP TO CARIS ASSURE: Caris Detect is a separate forthcoming screening product; Caris Assure is the existing therapy selection liquid biopsy. The July 2025 Scientific Reports publication (Nature journal) validated Caris Assure MCED capability with different performance numbers — do not conflate with Caris Detect Achieve 1 data.
fdaStatus: Pre-market; not yet commercially available. No FDA approval, clearance, or Breakthrough Device designation for Caris Detect. Note: Caris Assure (therapy selection) is a separate, commercially available CLIA-validated test. Caris Life Sciences is publicly traded (NASDAQ: CAI).
fdaStatusNotes: Caris Detect is a forthcoming MCED test expected to launch H1 2026. Achieve 1 finalized results (March 31, 2026) support commercial launch.

name: Harbinger MCED
method: Proprietary multi-analyte platform combining protein biomarkers and cfDNA analysis for multi-cancer detection
cancerTypes: 15+ tumor types including hepatobiliary, upper GI, colorectal, lung
targetPopulation: Adults at average risk for cancer
sensitivityNotes: CORE-HH prospective (ASCO 2025): 25.8% early-stage (I-II) at 98.3% specificity. Earlier 2023 pilot (case-control): 82% overall, 74% Stage I, 84% Stage II at 95% spec across 15 tumor types. Dramatic gap reflects case-control optimism vs prospective reality.
specificityNotes: CORE-HH prospective study specificity. 2023 pilot: 95%.
performanceNotes: Two datasets with dramatically different results. 2023 pilot (case-control): 82% overall sensitivity, 74% Stage I, 84% Stage II at 95% specificity across 15 tumor types (GenomeWeb). 2025 CORE-HH (ASCO, prospective): 25.8% early-stage (I-II) at 98.3% specificity, 80.3% late-stage (III-IV). Per-cancer PPV reported (hepatobiliary 15%, upper GI 22%, colorectal 33%, lung 25%) but per-cancer sensitivity not published.
commercialPayersNotes: Aetna: Not covered (CPB 0352). Aetna: denies (CPB 0352 — not covered under traditional tumor marker policy).
clinicalAvailability: Not commercially available; RESOLVE™ platform commercial launch expected H2 2026
clinicalAvailabilityNotes: Harbinger announced RESOLVE™ as new product category (April 2026) with $100M funding. PROCARES validation study supporting launch.
isInvestigationalNotes: Pre-commercial. No FDA submission announced. April 2026: Harbinger announced RESOLVE™ as new diagnostic platform category with $100M Flagship Pioneering funding; PROCARES validation study supporting commercial launch expected H2 2026.
technologyDifferentiator: Combines protein biomarkers with cfDNA analysis. Notable for the dramatic performance gap between case-control (82%) and prospective (25.8%) validation, illustrating the challenges of translating MCED performance to real-world screening.

name: Freenome Lung Cancer Blood Test
method: AI/ML analyzing base-resolution methylation sequencing of cfDNA combined with plasma protein immunoassays for lung cancer detection. Same multiomics platform as Freenome CRC test.
targetPopulation: Current and former smokers aged 50-80 eligible for LDCT screening
sensitivityNotes: Development data (March 2026): 90.7% adjusted sensitivity at 50% specificity; 80.4% at 75% specificity. Operating-point-based reporting does not map to single sensitivity value.
stageISensitivityNotes: 77.1% Stage I adjusted sensitivity at 50% specificity (development data).
specificityNotes: Sensitivity reported at fixed specificity thresholds (50% and 75%); standalone specificity not separately reported.
performanceNotes: Development data (March 2026, 673 samples: 363 lung cancer + 310 controls): 90.7% adjusted sensitivity at 50% specificity; 80.4% at 75% specificity; Stage I: 77.1% at 50% specificity. Detects adenocarcinoma, squamous cell, and small-cell subtypes. No PPV/NPV yet.
fdaStatusNotes: LDT launch planned H2 2026. PROACT LUNG validation data intended to support future PMA submission.
reimbursementNote: Pre-commercial; no pricing, CPT code, or coverage established.
clinicalAvailability: Investigational - not yet commercially available. LDT launch planned H2 2026.
clinicalTrials: NCT06122077 PROACT LUNG - Prospective multi-center observational validation study, up to 20,000 participants at up to 100 US sites, primary completion June 2026
totalParticipantsNotes: PROACT LUNG enrollment target up to 20,000. Current/former smokers age 50+ eligible for LDCT screening.
numPublicationsNotes: No peer-reviewed publications yet. Development data presented via press release March 18, 2026.
technologyDifferentiator: Same multiomics platform as Freenome CRC test (cfDNA methylation + proteins). Roche partnership for ex-US commercialization (~$200M deal, Nov 2025). NVIDIA partnership for AI/deep learning acceleration.
